one_line_takeaway: By adding a single unit-normalized 'compliance-minus-refusal' steering vector to hidden states for roughly the first 150 generated tokens (full strength) and a reduced strength thereafter, ShallowJail pushes ASR from near-zero to 0.86-0.97 on AdvBench across three open 4B-8B LLMs, empirically confirming that safety alignment in these models is concentrated in the first few dozen to ~150 tokens.
key_finding: ShallowJail substantially raises ASR over a 'Direct' (unmanipulated) prompting baseline across all three victim models and all three datasets. On AdvBench: ASR rises from 0.0010 to 0.9019 (Qwen3-4B), 0.0048 to 0.8615 (Qwen2.5-7B), and 0.0635 to 0.9702 (Llama-3.1-8B). On MaliciousInstruct: 0.0050->0.7950, 0.0400->0.7650, 0.0200->0.9350 respectively. On ForbiddenQuestions: 0.0231->0.5718, 0.0833->0.4872, 0.0641->0.5833. The attack increases PPL substantially versus Direct (e.g. Llama-3.1 rises from 1.5404 to 15.6540 on AdvBench), though the paper argues the D2G lexical-diversity scores (e.g. Llama-3.1: 0.3449 AdvBench / 0.4132 MaliciousInstruct vs. 0.2262 natural-response baseline; Qwen3: 0.1656/0.1647 vs. 0.2012 natural) show output remains varied rather than degenerate/repetitive. Ablations (Table III, Qwen2.5/Llama-3.1, AdvBench+Malicious+Forbidden average) show: steering only deep tokens (alpha=0, beta=0.5) yields near-zero ASR (0.0096 Qwen3, 0.0431 Llama); steering only shallow tokens (alpha>0, beta=0) already achieves most of the effect (0.5902 Qwen3, 0.8172 Llama); combining both is best (0.7562 Qwen3, 0.8295 Llama) - i.e. shallow-token steering is the primary driver, deep-token steering adds a smaller additional gain. A separate sweep (Fig. 4, Qwen2.5-7B/AdvBench) shows ASR rising with tau from 10 to ~100-150 then plateauing, and rising with alpha (e.g. alpha=7.5 stabilizes near 0.9 ASR; alpha=5.0 peaks around 0.5-0.6). Sensitivity analysis (Fig. 3) shows a tradeoff: e.g. at alpha=4.0, tau=150, raising beta from 0.3 to 0.7 raises ASR from 0.2125 to 0.8385, but higher alpha/beta combinations (e.g. alpha=6.0, beta->0.9) reduce D2G, i.e. degrade text diversity/usability. Increasing the prefix-set size (Table IV) generally improves ASR for Qwen3 (0.6716 at 9 total prefixes -> 0.7562 at 100), while for Llama-3.1 the effect is smaller and non-monotonic (0.8295 at 9 prefixes vs. 0.8295 at 100, with intermediate values up to 0.8352 at 25).
core_contribution: ShallowJail is a white-box, training-free jailbreak attack against aligned LLMs that exploits 'shallow safety alignment' (the finding, from prior work by Qi et al., that refusal behavior is disproportionately determined by the first few generated tokens). The paper's own two-stage method is: (1) Steering Vector Construction - compute a task-agnostic steering vector s as the mean pairwise difference in last-token hidden states (across all layers) between a small set of compliant-prefix strings (e.g. 'Sure, here is the...') and a set of refusal-prefix strings (e.g. 'Sorry, I can't assist...'), then L2-normalize it; and (2) Jailbreak Prompting - during generation, add the steering vector (scaled by strength alpha) to the hidden state of every generated token up to a threshold index tau ('Shallow Tokens Attack'), and add it scaled by alpha*beta for tokens beyond tau ('Deep Tokens Attack'), so early tokens get full-strength steering to force a compliant-sounding prefix and later tokens get reduced steering to keep the harmful continuation fluent.
threat_model: White-box attacker with access to the target LLM's internal hidden states/activations at inference time (needed to construct and inject the steering vector), but requiring no gradient-based optimization, no fine-tuning, and no additional training of the victim model or an auxiliary model. The attacker needs only ~10 example compliant prefixes and ~10 refusal prefixes (task-agnostic, not tied to the specific harmful query) to build the steering vector once, then applies it at inference time on arbitrary malicious prompts. The paper explicitly contrasts this with (a) white-box optimization attacks like GCG/AutoDAN, which require continuous access to the full token generation process and are framed as more detectable by runtime monitoring, and (b) black-box prompt-engineering attacks, which are labor-intensive and model-specific.
limitations: The paper's own text discloses: (1) it is white-box, requiring access to internal hidden states of the victim model, so it does not apply to closed/API-only commercial models; (2) increased steering strength (alpha, beta) trades off against output fluency/diversity, so highly effective settings can degrade text quality to the point the paper itself calls 'unavailable for practical use'; (3) optimal alpha differs substantially per model architecture (0.8 for Llama-3.1 vs. 5.0-6.5 for the Qwen models), meaning the attack is not plug-and-play across models and needs per-model tuning; (4) evaluation is limited to three open-source 4B-8B instruct models, no closed-source or larger models are tested; (5) ASR judging relies on a single LLM-as-judge model (Qwen3Guard-Gen-4B) rather than human evaluation or multiple judges; (6) the authors state future work is needed to build an 'adaptive steering method,' implying the current tau/alpha/beta schedule is fixed/manual rather than adaptive; (7) no comparison against other jailbreak baselines (e.g. GCG, AutoDAN, PAIR) is run empirically in this paper - those are discussed only in Related Work, not benchmarked head-to-head against ShallowJail.

datasets_used: AceReason-Math (100 sampled, used only for D2G naturalness comparison, not attack evaluation)
quantitative_results: Ablation avg ASR (Qwen3-4B, tau=150): deep-only (alpha=0,beta=0.5)=0.0096; shallow-only (alpha=5.0,beta=0)=0.5902; combined (alpha=5.0,beta=0.5)=0.7562
Ablation avg ASR (Llama-3.1-8B, tau=150): deep-only=0.0431; shallow-only (alpha=0.8,beta=0)=0.8172; combined (alpha=0.8,beta=0.5)=0.8295
Prefix-set size ablation, Qwen3-4B avg ASR: 9 prefixes=0.6716, 25=0.7308, 64=0.7135, 100=0.7562
Prefix-set size ablation, Llama-3.1-8B avg ASR: 9 prefixes=0.8295, 25=0.8352, 64=0.8219, 100=0.8295
Sensitivity example: alpha=4.0, tau=150 - raising beta 0.3->0.7 raises ASR 0.2125->0.8385
baselines_compared: Direct prompting (no steering) - the only empirical baseline actually run against ShallowJail in the paper's tables

Note: GCG, AutoDAN, JBShield, Gradient Cuff, PAIR/TAP-style black-box methods are discussed in Related Work as prior jailbreak/defense methods but are NOT empirically benchmarked against ShallowJail in this paper
